metadata.summary: AI-generated user profiles that simulate survey and interview responses for directional research. Always labeled synthetic; always followed by real-user validation before product decisions.

content.title: Synthetic Users
content.summary: AI-generated user profiles that simulate survey and interview responses for directional, pre-validation research. Always labeled as synthetic; always followed by real-user validation before any product decision. The methodology's primary value is hypothesis triage — identifying what is worth investing in real-user research time.

why.p: Real user research is slow and expensive. Synthetic users provide fast directional signal for early ideation and hypothesis filtering. The single greatest adoption barrier is a "crisis of trust" from data quality concerns; the methodology addresses this with mandatory governance rules and explicit use-case constraints.

list.item: Early ideation: generating directional signal before real users can be recruited.
Hypothesis stress-testing as a fast pre-filter ("Would this persona care about X?").
Low-stakes concept validation where the cost of being wrong is recoverable.
Generating adversarial edge-case responses to find blind spots in a survey instrument.
Producing a research brief baseline that a human researcher then corrects.
Go/no-go product decisions — synthetic data cannot replace real demand signal.
Demand forecasting or pricing research — synthetic willingness-to-pay is systematically biased high.
Any research where legal, medical, or safety consequences follow from findings.
Final validation before launch — always run at least one round of real-user interviews.
Stakeholder-facing deliverables presented as real research without disclosure.

section.name: what-are-synthetic-users
section.p: AI-generated profiles that mimic user groups, producing artificial research findings without studying real users. Useful for directional signal only; not a substitute for observational or behavioral data.

rule.statement: Use synthetic users for directional hypothesis triage; never for go/no-go decisions, demand forecasting, or final validation before launch.
rule.rationale: Synthetic responses skew positive and fail to surface genuine emotional friction. They cannot replace demand signal from real users with budget authority.

section.name: valid-vs-invalid-use-cases
section.p: Governance rules that define the scope and limits of synthetic user research.

rule.statement: Always label synthetic research outputs as "synthetic" in every document header; never present alongside real findings without clear separation.
rule.rationale: Mixed presentation misleads stakeholders into treating synthetic signal as real. The "crisis of trust" in synthetic research is driven largely by undisclosed synthetic data.

rule.statement: Follow synthetic research immediately with 3-5 real user interviews before acting on findings.
rule.rationale: Synthetic users identify what is worth investigating; real users validate whether the signal is genuine.

rule.statement: Never use synthetic research for a go/no-go decision without at least one round of human validation.
rule.rationale: Synthetic willingness-to-pay is systematically biased high; decisions based on it routinely overestimate demand.

rule.statement: Define a persona retirement schedule (every 6 months or after major market shifts); assign ownership for persona accuracy.
rule.rationale: Behavioral assumptions in synthetic profiles drift with market changes; unretired personas become organizational fiction.

rule.statement: Document the exact persona construction prompt; if results need to be reproduced or audited, the prompt is the methodology.
rule.rationale: Minor prompt changes produce different "findings." Without the exact prompt, results cannot be reproduced or challenged.

section.name: tool-landscape
section.p: Available tools and services for synthetic user research with varying API access and use-case focus.

rule.statement: Tool selection: Synthetic Users (syntheticusers.com) offers REST API for profile creation and survey response simulation; SyntheticAudience.ai provides quantitative research at scale with panel-style responses; Viewpoints.ai enables fast concept validation with limited API access; Aaru supports scenario testing and predictive analysis with strongest B2C focus; UserIntelligence provides simulation engine for directional insights; Qualtrics Edge Audience offers modeled audience layers requiring enterprise contract.
rule.rationale: Each tool has distinct strengths; API access, scale, and focus vary. Tool selection depends on research phase and team constraints.

section.name: key-limitations
section.p: Structural constraints that define when synthetic users add value and when they mislead.

rule.statement: Synthetic responses are sensitive to persona framing: minor prompt changes produce different "findings."
rule.rationale: Prompt sensitivity means results are not objective; the methodology is the prompt, not the tool.

rule.statement: Synthetic users provide no behavioral data — only stated preferences, which diverge from observed behavior.
rule.rationale: What people say they will do differs from what they actually do. Synthetic research captures articulated intent only.

rule.statement: Nonverbal signals (hesitation, tone, body language) are absent — a major loss for usability research.
rule.rationale: Emotional friction and genuine confusion are invisible in synthetic responses.

rule.statement: No industry standard for synthetic data quality; results cannot be independently audited.
rule.rationale: Synthetic research lacks third-party validation mechanisms; quality is internal-only.

rule.statement: Hallucination risk: synthetic users may report features or experiences that do not exist in the product.
rule.rationale: Synthetic profiles can fabricate capabilities or workflows based on training data rather than product reality.

rule.statement: Synthetic responses skew positive and fail to surface genuine emotional friction.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to cooperative, positive personas; real users bring frustration and edge cases.

section.name: agent-workflow-pipeline
section.p: Profile generation and response simulation pipeline with bias detection, gap analysis, and mandatory human checkpoints.

rule.statement: Generate at least 5-8 profiles to detect uniformity bias; fewer profiles amplify the LLM's default persona and reduce signal diversity.
rule.rationale: With fewer than 5 profiles, the model's prior dominates and all responses converge on the same stereotypical answers, which appear as "strong signal" but are actually LLM defaults.

rule.statement: Use adversarial prompting ("as a skeptical user, why would you NOT buy this?") to counteract the positive-response bias of synthetic profiles.
rule.rationale: Standard prompts elicit cooperative, positive synthetic responses. Adversarial framing forces the model to generate the friction signals that matter for product decisions.

rule.statement: Inject forced variation in the profile construction prompt to prevent all profiles from sharing the same frustrations.
rule.rationale: Without forced variation, profiles converge on common UX tropes ("too complex," "not enough integrations") that reflect LLM training data, not the actual ICP.

rule.statement: Flag topics where all profiles agree as low signal; flag topics with genuine divergence as high signal worth real-user follow-up.
rule.rationale: Uniform synthetic agreement means the LLM defaulted to a consensus answer; divergence indicates a real dimension of variation in the ICP worth investigating.

rule.statement: Do not chain synthetic-user output directly into downstream agents (copy generation, pricing models) without human review.
rule.rationale: Hallucinated preferences propagate through the pipeline and corrupt downstream outputs in ways that are hard to trace back to the synthetic source.

example.title: Profile generation with variation
example.code: Create 5 diverse synthetic user profiles for B2B marketing managers in SaaS companies. For each, vary: - Company size (10-50 vs 100-500 vs 500+) - Tech comfort level (1-5 scale) - Primary job: demand gen vs. product marketing vs. content - Budget authority (yes/no) - Top frustration: distinct for each profile
example.note: Explicit diversity constraints force variation; prevents uniform convergence.

example.title: Adversarial prompting
example.code: As [Profile Name], why would you NOT adopt this tool? - What would make it not worth the cost? - What would need to change for you to recommend it? - Where does it fall short compared to your current setup?
example.note: Adversarial framing surfaces friction and skepticism.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when executing synthetic user workflows with AI agents.

rule.statement: Synthetic users are LLM personas — they encode training data biases, not your actual ICP's behavior; treat as directional only.
rule.rationale: Synthetic profiles reflect LLM training data, not market reality.

rule.statement: Agents may produce profiles that all share the same "frustrations" because the LLM defaults to common UX tropes; inject forced variation in the prompt.
rule.rationale: Default LLM behavior produces stereotype responses.

rule.statement: Human-in-loop checkpoint required before synthetic findings influence product roadmap or budget decisions.
rule.rationale: Synthetic output must pass human judgment before resource allocation.

rule.statement: When an agent runs simulated interviews at scale (50+ profiles), the diversity of responses actually decreases — the LLM regresses to mean; use smaller batches with explicit diversity constraints.
rule.rationale: Scale reduces diversity; smaller batches with constraints preserve signal quality.

rule.statement: Do not chain synthetic-user output directly into downstream agents (e.g., copy generation, pricing models) without human review; hallucinated preferences will propagate.
rule.rationale: Synthetic data quality degrades when passed through multiple agent stages without verification.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Practical patterns for high-quality synthetic user research within governance constraints.

rule.statement: Always label synthetic research outputs as "synthetic" in any document header; never present alongside real findings without clear separation.
rule.rationale: Labeling prevents misrepresentation and maintains trust.

rule.statement: Generate at least 5-8 profiles to detect uniformity bias; fewer profiles amplify the LLM's default persona.
rule.rationale: Adequate sample size prevents false-signal convergence.

rule.statement: Use adversarial prompting ("as a skeptical user, why would you NOT buy this?") to counteract positive bias.
rule.rationale: Adversarial framing surfaces friction and critical signals.

rule.statement: Follow synthetic research immediately with 3-5 real user interviews before acting on findings.
rule.rationale: Real-user validation is the gateway to confident decision-making.

rule.statement: Document the exact persona construction prompt — if results need to be reproduced or audited, the prompt is the methodology.
rule.rationale: Prompt transparency enables reproducibility and auditability.

rule.statement: Retire personas every 6 months or after major market shifts; behavioral assumptions drift.
rule.rationale: Stale personas become organizational fiction.

template.purpose: Python: profile generation and simulated interview via Anthropic SDK.
Prompt template for generating synthetic user profiles with anti-uniformity constraints.
